preamble: The transformation from a voiced retroflex affricate /ɖʐ/ to a voiceless postalveolar affricate /tʃ/ primarily involves devoicing, a common phonological process. While the immediate outcome of devoicing a retroflex affricate is typically a voiceless retroflex affricate /ʈʂ/, historical developments and cross-linguistic variations in Slavic languages demonstrate a tendency for retroflex articulations to correspond to or shift towards postalveolar ones. This shift often involves a reduction in the degree of retroflexion, moving towards a more fronted postalveolar articulation.
phoneticEffects: Devoicing (loss of vocal fold vibration), de-retroflexion (a shift from retroflex to postalveolar place of articulation, often involving a reduction in the degree of retroflexion), fortition (maintaining the affricate manner of articulation)

examples.note: Word-final devoicing of the voiced retroflex affricate to a voiceless retroflex affricate, which is phonetically closely related to the voiceless postalveolar affricate /tʃ/ and corresponds to it in other Slavic languages.

In contrast to Polish, Czech historically developed or retained the voiceless postalveolar affricate /tʃ/ in positions corresponding to where Polish features retroflex affricates like /ɖʐ/ or /ʈʂ/, illustrating a historical divergence or a shift in the precise place of articulation within Slavic languages.

sources: Hamann, S. (2003). Postalveolar Fricatives in Slavic Languages as Retroflexes. In J. van de Weijer, V. van Heuven & H. van der Hulst (Eds.), Phonetic Sciences (pp. 107-126). LOT.
Żygis, M. (2005). (Non)Retroflexivity of Slavic Affricates and Its Motivation. Evidence from Polish and Czech <č>.
Hamann, S. & Rochoit, M. (2003). The Evolution of Sibilants in Polish and Russian. ZAS Papers in Linguistics, 32, 155-174.

Krynicki, G., Wypych, M., & Jarek, W. (2022). Are Polish affricates retroflex? A cross-linguistic electropalatographic study with a redesigned Reading-type palate. Alkalmazott Nyelvtudomány, XXII(1), 223–242.
